data.title: SSR Mining: Still Undervalued Despite The Rally
data.description:
Introduction

Since my previous article about SSR Mining (NASDAQ:SSRM)(TSX:SSRM:CA) titled "SSR Mining: Sitting On A Gold Mine... Literally. Hidden Value In Plain Sight" was published at the end of July, the stock is up about 82% now thanks especially to gold jumping to a new all-time high.

However, the company itself is yet to show the improvements I mentioned as potential catalysts, and I believe the stock price jump is mostly the result of gold’s move (macro factors), while the company is still sitting on a literal gold mine awaiting a restart.

Latest Quarter

To summarize, Q2 was overall quite an amazing quarter for SSRM, with $98.38 million in free cash flow, a more than doubled gold production compared to Q2 2024, as well as an even better financial position with $182.1 million in net cash and $912.1 million in liquidity available. Of course, higher gold prices played an essential role in this, but the acquisition of CC&V and their other developments also played a very important part, while H2 has potential to be even better based on the guidance mentioned below.

The company’s guidance remained very solid, with a ~10% YoY increase in gold equivalent ounce production and continued investments in the Hod Maden project. They also highlighted the expanded life of their Puna silver mine in Argentina, with initial plans to add another 3 years of silver production. Again, the company remains a very solid gold, silver and copper producer especially once the Hod Maden project will become operational, with a very diversified asset portfolio for their size, a lot of North American exposure plus the Çöpler mine that could potentially restart following the tragic accident that occurred back in February 2024, which was a major source of revenue for them.

The company maintained their Çöpler mine restart as a top priority, although a timeline for restart approval is not available for now. As the Executive Chairman said during the Q2 earnings Q&A, they’re making progress and believe that they’re on the right track, although no timeline can yet be provided:

So that's been a good quarter for us actually in a lot of ways to get a lot of the open issues resolved. So that's favorable. And then as we look forward, there isn't a sort of set of things that we have to do per se, Ovais, you must do this before you do that. Clearly, we want to make sure that everything is in place before we seek approval for a restart but we're not quite there in a few regards, but we're obviously plugging along and making good progress. So I was really pleased with the quarter, actually.

However, when this happens, I believe we’re very likely looking at a potentially significant jump in stock price, as the market didn’t seem to account for this mine ever becoming operational again, back when I looked into them. They also said that a potential restart will begin with a revert in permits to the 2014 EIA, which is around 6,000 tonnes per day, but progress from that point could later be achieved over time, which is an important aspect to keep in mind. This means the mine won’t be as big as it was before at first, but its lifetime could also be extended this way by lowering its production and later unlocking its growth.

On top of that, their Hod Maden project advanced as planned, which is a massive growth catalyst that comes on top of the Çöpler mine’s potential restart. While the latter isn’t guaranteed, Hod Maden is a project that they’re developing and expecting first production around 2028, being a key part of the company’s growth pipeline. Still, they have $912.1 million in liquidity available, which should help them take care of virtually most if not all costs that could potentially arise from developing these two projects, although the legal risk in Çöpler remains unknown, to the point where there could be no recovery ever.

Macro Changes

I believe most of the stock’s recent jump has little to do with the company itself and is rather the effect of gold reaching a new all-time high, combined with their results and potential. Of course, it shouldn’t be a surprise that the stock is up so much when gold itself is up ~40% YTD, which is an amazing performance for what’s generally considered a slow commodity that’s seen as a hedge. However, when the factors that gold is used as a hedge against become more likely, it’s no wonder that the commodity outperforms.

The main reason behind gold’s jump were rate cut expectations, especially following Powell’s Jackson Hole remarks, which were seen as very bullish for gold since they made a September cut very likely in the market’s opinion, as we can see. If done right and not rushed to a point where it can shock the markets, rate cuts have a boosting effect on the economy thanks to lowering the cost of debt, which in turn leads to more transactions, investments, higher discretionary budgets, etc. However, they also push money into other assets, including outside of the US, which can then weaken the dollar, which would be a double-win for gold mining stocks, at least. If we think about tariffs and the uncertainty seen on top at the same time, it’s honestly no surprise that gold prices rose so much lately.

Something else worth mentioning, which I also noted in the B2Gold (BTG) updated analysis posted recently, is Tether’s development into gold mining, which I believe could be another potential to bring attention to gold and gold mining stocks. I say that because we could be looking at a potentially new source of demand, as this “new market” coming from crypto buyers doesn’t generally have common ground with gold investors, and these two assets are not that often seen together in the same portfolio. What this move can do is bring some common ground and especially attention to gold, which continued to breach its all-time high and could get into FOMO, hype, and other market phenomena, especially if inflation/hard landing/recession fears rise - which are part of why I think gold’s upside potential is nearly unlimited, especially when we consider its unique sources of demand (jewelry/investing/banks representing ~90% of the demand). Also, earlier this year, Tether expanded their stake into my largest position - Adecoagro (AGRO) - from ~20% to ~70%, which I believe is a very interesting development that could shine a new light on commodities going forward.

Updated Valuation

As stated in the initial analysis, the previous valuation based on gold below ~$3,000 per ounce in the long-term that takes ZERO production from Çöpler into account and also assumes costs generated by it returned a fair value estimate of ~$20.43. While the stock is now even trading a bit above that target level, we also have to take into account the potential for higher gold prices going forward.

Although we can’t know the exact amount of money they can make at each price of gold since there are fixed and variable costs associated with it (miners offer leverage on gold), if we assume the $137.68 million in FCF they made in H1 2025 (of which $98.38 million came in Q2 alone) as a starting point, which came during average gold prices of ~$3032.5 (this is the average gold price during H1 2025; $3,151/oz average realized gold price) and increase it by only ~10%, so basically lifting the long-term expected gold price to ~$3,335, give or take, which I believe is a fair average assumption for 2025 at least. On the same level of CAPEX on average and with a 1-to-1 effect on the cash flow (it’s usually more, but just being conservative), we’re looking at ~$303 million in free cash flow per year at this level.

Once again, this FCF is then projected to grow at a 4% CAGR for the first 5 years followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years. Assuming a 2% terminal growth rate and a WACC of 9%, slightly above my estimated 8.84% for a better margin of safety (assumptions: ~9.2% cost of equity, ~4.2% cost of debt) and considering $182.1 million in net cash on 202.8 million shares outstanding, discounting the cash flows and terminal value gives an enterprise value of $6.15 billion, which, after adding net cash, implies an equity value of $6.33 billion, or ~$31.22 per share, so still some good upside left.

Again, this is without Çöpler, and a potential restart even at a much lower production rate would come as a massive bonus, so the stock is still trading below what I’d consider to be a fair value in this environment.

Conclusions

Overall, even when not taking SSRM’s Çöpler mine into account, the potential remains quite significant, backed by a very solid balance sheet with a net cash position and strong cash flows, plus potential from copper and silver on top of gold. I believe SSRM remains a Buy now despite the significant jump, as gold, silver and copper prices are likely to keep being supported by long-term tailwinds, even if prices won’t remain at all-time highs forever, of course.

data.title: Constellation Energy: Strategic M&A And Secular Demand Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying shares of Constellation Energy Corporation (NASDAQ:CEG). My investment idea is based on the company's apparent undervaluation as the largest producer of clean energy in the United States with long-term growth drivers. Thanks to government support (PTC loans and the OBBBA Act), promising projects (the contract with Meta, the restart of the Crane nuclear power plant) and the upcoming large-scale acquisition of Calpine, CEG will be able to increase financial results in the coming years.

Despite steady profit growth, the company's multipliers remain moderate, which creates an upside of 9.5% to my target price of $353. In other words, the market does not fully consider the potential of CEG, and the current risks do not outweigh the expected benefits for shareholders.

The thesis focuses on the conviction that CEG shares are undervalued and their growth will continue in 2026, supported by stable fundamental trends.

Strategic Deals Reinforce Competitive Edge

Constellation Energy is a leading American electric power company specializing in the production of carbon-free energy. The company's business is twofold:

  1. Generation, which includes 20 GW of nuclear capacity, as well as wind, solar, hydro and gas plants;
  2. A commercial sales division that sells electricity and gas to industrial, commercial and residential customers throughout the country.

CEG earns money by selling its produced energy on the wholesale market and to end users, benefiting from its low cost of nuclear generation and long-term contracts. In addition, the company receives additional income through clean energy support mechanisms - in the form of government loans PTC (Production Tax Credit) and regional subsidies ZEC (Zero Emission Credits), which increases the marginality of nuclear power plants.

In the second quarter of 2025, Constellation Energy signed a 20-year contract with Meta for the supply of all energy from the Clinton nuclear power plant. At the same time, the company received regulatory approvals for the purchase of Calpine, one of the largest operators of gas and renewable plants. Against this background, the rules of the game for the industry are also shaping up in its favor: in the summer, the OBBBA law was passed, which extends tax credits for existing nuclear power plants until 2032 and additionally supports new nuclear projects. All this together strengthens Constellation's position in the market and creates the basis for further growth.

Revenue Momentum vs. Cost Headwinds in Q2 2025

CEG's financial results for the second quarter of 2025 confirm the steady growth of the business. Revenue for the quarter increased by +11% year-on-year and reached $6.101 billion. The growth was driven by several factors: firstly, sales volumes and average electricity prices in key regions increased. In addition, across the entire geography, CEG was able to sell more electricity at higher contract prices and effectively implemented hedging: in the Mid-Atlantic region alone, hedging added $100 million to revenue in the second quarter. The retail segment (sales to businesses and households) also grew by about $110 million in the same Mid-Atlantic due to increased tariffs and demand. Thus, the overall revenue trend is positive - in the first half of 2025, it increased to $12.889 billion (+10.8% YoY), reflecting increased demand for electricity and the successful operation of the commercial area.

The operating margin decreased to 15.6% from 20.1% a year earlier. This decrease in profitability is explained by the outstripping cost growth. CEG faced a sharp increase in the cost of fuel and energy purchases (+36.6% YoY, to $3.132 billion), mainly due to expensive gas and the need to purchase additional electricity on the market to fulfill contracts during scheduled shutdowns of nuclear power plants. Fuel and purchase costs also increased in other regions (by 21% in the Midwest, by 35% in Texas) . Thus, the decline in EBIT is due to the temporary compression of the spread between selling prices and fuel costs. If it were not for the increase in energy costs, operating profit would have increased along with revenue.

Despite the decrease in operating profit, net income for shareholders in the second quarter increased to $839 million (GAAP) from $814 million a year earlier. Accordingly, GAAP diluted EPS increased to $2.67 from $2.58. The main reason is positive other income. CEG received large unrealized gains on investments (for example, an increase in the cost of the nuclear decommissioning fund and other financial income), which increased accounting profits. The non-GAAP result is more indicative: Adjusted Operating Earnings reached $599 million, or $1.91 per share, up 14% from $1.68 a year earlier. The adjusted net profit margin in the second quarter was 9.8% (up from 9.1% a year ago), demonstrating that CEG's business is generating higher profitability due to increased revenue and control over fixed costs.

Q3 2025 Set to Outperform on Peak Demand

Officially, CEG is targeting a range of $8.90-$9.60 Adjusted EPS for the full year 2025, which is equivalent to an average of $2.3-$2.5 per share for each of the remaining quarters. Management did not directly disclose the forecast for the third quarter of 2025, however, taking into account the seasonal factor (summer peak of electricity consumption) and continuing positive trends, we can expect a significant increase in results. The company usually earns the highest profit in summer and winter, when demand and prices for electricity are at their maximum.

In my opinion, CEG in the third quarter will be able to increase revenue to $7 billion (against $6.3 billion in the third quarter of 2024) and achieve adjusted earnings of about $2.3-2.5 per share. The company's management noted the growing demand from data centers, electric vehicles and industry - these factors should support sales in the third quarter. In addition, savings from optimization measures will begin to show up in July: CEG completed an IT systems modernization program in the first half of the year and continues integration activities related to the future merger, which will gradually reduce unit costs. My forecast for Q3 2025 is revenue of $7.0 billion, adjusted EBITDA of about $1.3 billion, and Adjusted EPS of about $2.40. This will surpass the result of a year ago and pave the way for the fulfillment of the upper limit of the annual plan.

Valuation Supports Upside Potential

My estimate is based on a DCF model with an unlevered FCF for the period 2025-2030. For the forecast, I envision revenue growth of 5% CAGR to $30 billion by 2026, which will be facilitated by the restart of the Crane unit and the integration of Calpine. The operating margin, according to my expectations, will grow from 11% in 2025 to 12% in 2026 due to lower fuel price pressures and synergies from the transaction, which will increase EBIT to $3.4 billion. Capital expenditures will remain high (10% of revenue, or $2.5-3 billion per year), but the share of investments will gradually decrease after the completion of major projects. Net working capital in the model is neutral, as the increase in accounts receivable will be offset by inventory optimization and customer prepayments.

I discounted the forecast FCF for WACC at 8.59% due to the high share capital and the five-year beta at 1.12 (all other prerequisites are shown in the table above).

At first glance, the shares are trading at a premium to the sector: the current 2025E P/E is around 34x, and the 2025E EV/EBITDA is at 18-20x. These figures are higher than those of traditional utility companies, but a direct comparison is incorrect - CEG has much higher profit dynamics. Investors also underestimate CEG's ability to generate free cash flow: with CAPEX stabilizing and working capital declining, CEG will be able to spend about $1.5-2 billion annually on buyback and dividends without compromising its credit rating. Given the company's leading position in clean energy and guaranteed government support (at least until 2032), the current multipliers do not look too high. On the contrary, in my opinion, CEG shares are trading at a discount to their fair value.

Stock valuation based on terminal value calculation multipliers through P/E and EV/EBITDA. If we look at the valuation through P/E, then with a forward multiplier of 34x, the fair price is about $353 per share. This is about 9.5% higher than the current level. The EV/EBITDA method shows a similar result. Even if we make more restrained assumptions, estimates are still higher than the current price, and in optimistic scenarios, the growth potential goes beyond 15%. In fact, this means that the premium to the sector is justified — the company has clear profit growth drivers and high predictability of future cash flows. Taking into account the combination of the fundamental strength of the business and positive ratings on both methods, I assign a Buy rating to Constellation Energy shares with a target price of $353 and an expected upside of 9.5%.

Debt Comfort, Accelerated Buybacks

Constellation Energy adheres to a balanced policy regarding debt and return of funds to shareholders. The net debt is just over $6 billion, with almost $2 billion in cash on the balance sheet. CEG has a moderate debt burden: the Net Debt/EBITDA ratio is about 1.5x, which is very comfortable for a company with stable cash flows.

Thanks to stable revenue and government support, CEG generates significant free cash and actively shares it with shareholders. In the second quarter of 2025, the company accelerated its buyback program: $400 million worth of shares were repurchased through accelerated buybacks. In total, $404 million was allocated for repurchase in the first half of 2025, which reduced the number of outstanding shares by 1% (to 312.4 million shares).

In parallel, CEG increases its dividend payments. According to management's promise, the annual dividend in 2025 has been increased by +10% - the quarterly payout has increased to $0.3878 per share. The dividend yield remains modest (0.5% per annum) due to the strong growth in the share price, but the dividend policy itself is important: the company is committed to an annual increase in payments by 10% in the coming years. This signals confidence in future cash flows. In total, for 2023-1025 CEG returned almost $2 billion to shareholders through dividends and buyback, fulfilling promises after the spin-off. A continuation of this strategy is expected ahead: upon completion of the Calpine takeover, the company intends to temporarily suspend major M&A and focus on integration, which will redirect significant FCF to shareholder payments.

Risks

First, there is the market and price risk: As a competitive electricity producer, CEG is dependent on fluctuations in electricity and fuel prices. Although federal PTC loans insure nuclear generation against extremely low prices, under the scenario of a prolonged excess of capacity in the markets (PJM, ERCOT), the company's profits will suffer. Secondly, there is a regulatory risk: CEG's business benefits from the support of the nuclear industry (IRA/PTC, ZEC programs). There is also a risk of stricter environmental regulations for nuclear power plants, which will increase the cost of safety and waste disposal. Thirdly, operational risks: the operation of nuclear power plants requires impeccable safety. An unplanned reactor shutdown due to technical reasons or, worse, an incident is not only a direct loss and repair costs, but also a reputational blow. In addition, large-scale expansion at the expense of Calpine carries an integration risk: the merger of two large companies may be accompanied by difficulties in synchronizing business processes, cultural and personnel problems. If the synergies turn out to be less than expected, profit growth forecasts may not be justified.

Final Thoughts

Constellation Energy offers a rare combination of stability and growth in the electric power industry. Yes, the company has high multipliers, but they are justified by double-digit profit growth rates and CEG's strong position as a leader in the transition to clean energy. The risks taken - market volatility, acquisition integration - look moderate and manageable. At the same time, CEG has long-term advantages: guaranteed support for nuclear generation until the 2030s, scale, technological leadership and financial discipline. The investment thesis is that the benefits outweigh the risks: Constellation shares should continue to grow as strategic initiatives are implemented.

data.title: Virtus KAR Emerging Markets Small-Cap Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

The second quarter started with a wave of new U.S. tariffs, which caused market volatility to rise and stocks to decline sharply. However, the quarter ended with a strong market rally after earlier tariffs were suspended and trade negotiations progressed. Recession fears also receded.

Information technology and communication services were among the top-performing sectors in the benchmark MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index, led by renewed enthusiasm around artificial intelligence (AI). AI enthusiasm also drove Taiwanese equities higher. Regionally, Korea was one of the strongest performers in the quarter as political instability subsided following the election of a new president. Brazil also continued to perform strongly driven by easing inflation, attractive valuation, and an expected interest rate cut. Thailand, on the other hand, continued to underperform amid political instability and slower economic growth. India also underperformed the broader benchmark due to concerns about growth and high valuations. Emerging markets continued to benefit from the weakness in the U.S. dollar. After underperforming in the first quarter, emerging markets small-cap equities outperformed their larger-cap counterparts.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR Emerging Markets Small-Cap Fund returned 13.11% (Class I)(MUTF:VIESX) in the quarter, underperforming the MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index, which returned 17.17%. Our underweight position in Taiwan was one of the larger detracting factors from the Fund’s performance during the quarter as the Taiwanese market rose strongly on AI enthusiasm. Our position in Korea was also a larger detractor as benchmark performance was driven by value-type names, such as holding companies and banks, on expectations of improved corporate governance that investors believe will lead to higher shareholder returns. Our portfolio is less exposed to this theme. Lastly, several of our portfolio companies in China underperformed.

Baltic Classifieds Group and Leeno Industrial were the largest contributors to performance in the quarter.

  • Listed in London, Baltic Classifieds Group operates several online classified portals across the Baltic states of Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia. The company has been reporting solid business results, which we believe contributed to the strong performance of the shares.
  • Leeno Industrial is a South Korea-based technology company that manufactures small pins and sockets used in testing the conductivity in semiconductor chip manufacturing. The stock outperformed as the company reported solid business results.

Union Auction and Anhui Gujing Distillery were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • Union Auction, a leading operator of used car auctions in Thailand, underperformed as a moratorium on debt collection led to fewer vehicle repossessions and lower auction volumes.
  • Anhui Gujing is the leading baijiu distillery in China’s Anhui province. While the company continues to outperform the broader baijiu industry, continued economic weakness is resulting in decelerating demand, and a recently introduced policy, curbing baijiu consumption at government employee banquets, has further impacted industry sentiment.

data.title: GBTC Is Growing AUM Again - Is Bitcoin Getting Better Than Gold?
data.description:
Between the end of 2024 and earlier this year, I covered most Bitcoin ETFs as part of my “Bitcoin ETFs Showdown” series. Back then, I found the Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:GBTC) to be a sub-par choice, given its hefty expense ratio and deteriorating liquidity metrics.

Yet, to my surprise, GBTC has actually seen an increase in popularity. After losing its crown as the most capitalized Bitcoin ETF earlier in 2025, its Assets Under Management [AUM] have increased again in the last few months. This is illustrated in my table below (column “AUM Δ P6M), with data updated as of July 2025.

This trend fits the overall growth of Bitcoin ETFs, which have reached record AUMs last month. This is what I will cover in this article, coming back to GBTC as a potential vehicle for investment, together with its “little brother”, the Grayscale Bitcoin Mini Trust ETF (BTC).

The Gold vs. Bitcoin debate: my thoughts

Bitcoin ETFs have shown steady growth in AUM since their launch. To the point that their total AUM is now approaching that of gold ETFs (see chart below).

Does this mean Bitcoin is taking over gold? Let me be clear - I do not think Bitcoin and Gold are mutually exclusive. I believe there is more than enough room for both assets, especially considering the current state of the US and world economy, with rampant currency debasement, fiscal recklessness and a return to lower interest rates.

Merely from a technical standpoint, I do believe that Bitcoin is superior to gold - something I covered extensively in my past articles about the cryptocurrency. Bitcoin has better durability (as it doesn’t decay), portability (moving gold from country to country can prove very difficult), divisibility (it can be divided in 100 million parts) and verifiability (it cannot be faked).

Bitcoin has roughly the same fungibility of gold but, more importantly, it is scarcer than gold by a completely different order of magnitude. Gold is present in nature at just above 3 parts per million, while only 21 millions Bitcoin will ever exist.

On the scale of humanity’s history, I do believe gold is set to eventually become obsolete. The moment sufficient advancements in technology will allow us to access gold in space, gold will have lost its scarcity relative to humanity’s scale. If humanity ever evolves to become a multi-planetary species, I cannot see that being compatible with using gold as a reserve asset.

However, within our lifetimes, I think both gold and Bitcoin will be able to thrive in the current geopolitical and macro context.

The state of Bitcoin: BTC ETFs inflows at odds with BTC on exchanges

The fact Bitcoin possesses great technical characteristics does not mean, of course, it will actually replace or match gold in market capitalization - not within our lifetime, nor possibly ever. For that to happen, institutions and retail investors alike actually need to buy and hold Bitcoin long term, believing in the investment thesis.

And here’s why I find the new AUM highs as confirming for my bullish thesis: this proves that interest is out there and more and more people decide to hold BTC. This is compatible with Bitcoin maturing as a global reserve asset (and not a currency).

However, this trend may appear to go against other trends that I have observed and discussed in my past coverage of BTC.

Most of all, the decline of Bitcoin held in exchanges. Bitcoin exchange balance has been on a steady decline since the launch of Bitcoin ETFs (see chart below). This means that increasingly more Bitcoin is held in self storage on hard or paper wallets than on crypto exchanges.

This is a trend that I find as somewhat puzzling, considering how most BTC ETFs use Coinbase, Inc. (COIN) as custodian, meaning that (at least in theory) their Bitcoins should appear on exchanges.

I personally explain this apparent paradox with the following dynamics:

  • The rate of holders switching from exchanges (or ETFs) to self custody outpaces ETF inflows.

  • Some of the Bitcoin held by ETF custodians is moved outside of the exchanges in cold storage by the custodians themselves, and may not appear in these charts.

  • Some Bitcoin ETFs use self custody rather than a custodian, and their wallets may at least partially appear as off exchanges.

What matters, however, is that both dynamics are bullish in the context of Bitcoin maturing into a global reserve asset: more inflows into ETFs showcase retail (and institutional) interest. Less Bitcoin on exchanges indicates long term holders are prevailing short term traders, and may even signal an incoming supply shock. While I find the latter dynamic the more bullish one, I welcome ETF inflows as a sign of maturity of Bitcoin.

This article is not focused only on Bitcoin, therefore I am not going to go further with my full investment thesis. I do however invite curious readers to go through my extensive coverage of this cryptocurrency on Seeking Alpha.

Risks of investing in Bitcoin today

Bitcoin remains a volatile asset, and it is worth noting it underperformed gold YTD. As I stated, even if Bitcoin has all the technical characteristics to become a global reserve asset, this is far from a certainty in itself.

I think investors should only be exposed to Bitcoin to the extent they can tolerate high volatility, and be very wary of the chance that Bitcoin remains “stuck” at levels near where it is today for the foreseeable future.

In my past Bitcoin coverage, I did discuss a bearish thesis on Bitcoin that sees it remaining a sort of “online casino”, with its only value derived from volatility and short term trading. This outcome is still a possibility at the time of writing and should be kept in mind by investors.

Conclusion: Should you invest in GBTC (and the BTC ETF)?

When I analyze investment funds, I always try to go back to the fundamental reason why they were launched in the first place. Among Bitcoin ETFs, GBTC has one of the most peculiar stories. The fund was launched in 2013 as a private, open-ended fund that invested in Bitcoin.

Back then, GBTC represented the only way to gain exposure to Bitcoin via a listed instrument in the stock market. It was also a way to gain exposure to Bitcoin while being technically under the SEC jurisdiction.

Given that regulatory framework, a somewhat hefty management fee was justified. Today, with a dozen more Bitcoin ETFs to choose from, it is difficult for new investors to justify being exposed to an ETF that costs more than five times its peers. GBTC has started slightly lagging behind its competition since last year, as shown by the chart below.

Unsurprisingly, GBTC has lost much of its AUM since its conversion to an ETF. Not last because many investors were in GBTC to cash out its (now long gone) mNAV discount pre-ETF conversion.

Today, GBTC keeps existing as is because, in my view, it provides a good revenue stream for its issuer, Grayscale. Rather than simply lowering its expense ratio to match rivals, Grayscale has decided to launch an alternative Bitcoin ETF to be competitive on the market. This is the Grayscale Bitcoin Mini Trust ETF.

A plateau of AUM for GBTC of is understandable, as people may still stay invested for different reasons:

  1. To avoid having to pay capital gains.

  2. For lack of access to other funds in some specific situations (e.g., retirement accounts managed by Grayscale).

  3. For ignorance about better options or simple laziness.

Personally, I once again find myself recommending other funds for Bitcoin exposure, due to GBTC’s hefty expense ratio at 1.50%. These include BTC, the “little brother” of GBTC, with its competitive 0.15% expense ratio, as well as the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), my favorite Bitcoin ETF. However, for investors who already have GBTC in their portfolio and fall within the first two cases I mentioned above, I recommend a HOLD.

data.title: Why Tesla Stock Is A Strong Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Remarkably, Tesla Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) remains one of the most prominent and possibly most misunderstood battleground stocks, still, after all these years. It's essentially like politics. There doesn't seem to be much middle ground on Tesla's stock. Investors either love it or hate it, and this dynamic is not likely to change any time soon.

Now, I've been an adamant Tesla bull for about 12 years now. I first started investing in the company's stock in October 2013. Tesla's stock has skyrocketed by about 3,500% since then, making it one of my top stock investments in my 23-year investment career.

In fact, I've discussed publicly that Tesla would very likely become a trillion-dollar company as far back as 2017. The company actually achieved the trillion-dollar milestone more rapidly than I anticipated.

Why I Have Upgraded Tesla Stock To A Strong Buy Now

I published my most recent bullish Tesla article in July with a Buy rating, and the stock has appreciated by approximately 30% since then. However, there was still a bit of uncertainty regarding CEO Elon Musk and Tesla then.

We were just coming out of the political storm, the tariff tantrum debacle, the incident with the now infamous Jeffry Epstein tweet had just occurred, and while there were still many questions surrounding the company, I remained bullish on Tesla's stock.

For the record. Tesla is my most significant portfolio position, accounting for roughly 13% of my portfolio's weight. I began increasing my Tesla holdings substantially in the $150-220 range in mid-2024, as the stock was getting no respect around this period.

However, given the recent developments, like the proposed $1 trillion pay package for Elon, Musk's purchase of a billion-dollars worth of Tesla's stock, improvements in FSD (Full Self-Drive), increased production output in Germany, and other constructive near, intermediate, and long term elements, I am upgrading Tesla to a strong buy now.

Why An $8.5 Trillion Market Cap Is No Fairy Tale

I discussed Tesla appreciating to a trillion dollar market cap in my first public Tesla article back in 2017 (I've published about 85 over the years). Many analysts argued that the company was grossly overvalued when its market cap was around $50 billion then. Well, provided that Tesla hit a market cap of around $1.5 trillion recently, it's increased about 30-fold since then. Therefore, going from $1.5 trillion to $8.5 trillion is only about a 5.7X increase. So, I don't see why it wouldn't be achievable given Tesla's enormous growth potential in various industries.

In fact, Elon Musk's proposed $1 trillion pay package is essentially contingent on the dynamic that Tesla's market cap must expand to about $8.5 trillion or higher within a ten-year time frame.

Deepwater Asset Management analyst Gene Munster, Dan Ives, Kathie Wood don't think that this is any kind of fairy tale, and neither do I. Let's consider several factors here.

Tesla Is A Dominant, Market Leading Tech Company

First and foremost, as I said in my interview eight years ago with Cheddar, Tesla is not a car company. It's a dominant, market leading technology company that sells cars.

However, this doesn't mean that Tesla's vehicle segment will be its most prominent business indefinitely. In fact, I believe that FSD/AI and robotics could be more significant, especially profitability wise within the next five years.

Additionally, Tesla's cars are not ordinary EVs. They could be perceived as robots on wheels, as they can drive themselves, and do other things that many other cars cannot.

What differentiates Tesla vehicles from other cars is their integration of software, hardware, and ecosystem features. I think Joe Rogan said it well, that Tesla essentially makes other cars feel stupid.

Tesla's Enormous FSD and AI Potential

Tesla's cars have multiple cameras that continuously record everything and ping some of the data back to Tesla's supercomputer, integrating its vehicles and the enormous amount of data into Tesla's broader ecosystem.

Tesla has one of the most advanced AI/FSD programs, and some analysts, including Dan Ives, myself, and others believe the FSD segment alone could easily become a trillion dollar market cap business for Tesla within the next several years.

Tesla Robotaxis are becoming a common sight, as the company continues improving its FSD capabilities. There is now an unreleased FSD version operational in Robotaxis, which does not require supervision from the driver.

We can only expect the technology to continue improving from here, in my view. Elon believes that Tesla will have autonomous ride hailing in at least half of the United States by the end of the year.

Tesla now has approval to operate in Texas, the Bay Area, and Nevada. So, this may very well turn into a domino effect. Therefore, Tesla's FSD business can potentially start delivering considerably more revenues and profitability sooner than many analysts expect.

Robotics Will Very Likely Be The Next Massive Business

Tesla's robots appear to be the best out of anything robotic-related I have ever seen. The Optimus - Gen 2 seems pretty impressive, but it's likely nothing compared to what Gen 3 will be. Also, consider that Tesla will incorporate AI capabilities with robotics. Yes, xAI's Grok in a robot. You don't think there will be a massive market for these things?

One of the reasons why Tesla has done so well with EVs, energy generation and storage, which by the way on its own could be worth about $200 billion, and other segments, is because the company always looks ahead, well ahead of the competition. And AI integrated robotics is the next massive market in my view.

Elon believes that about 80% of Tesla's future value could come from the Optimus segment. Some claim the Optimus business could be worth at least $4 trillion, but ultimately, it could be worth more than that, as Tesla shifts away from EVs and focuses more on robotics and AI.

Citi's (C) analyst believes the humanoid robotics business could be worth $7 trillion in the future, and Morgan Stanley (MS) thinks it can be a $5 trillion market, by 2050.

Now, of course, 2050 is a long time from now, but given Tesla's market leading position in this segment, the "future" could arrive sooner than expected. Thus, I don't see why Tesla's humanoid robot business can't be worth about $1 trillion or more in the next 5-10 years, potentially even sooner in a more bullish case scenario.

Technically Speaking

Technically speaking, Tesla's stock is overbought in the near term. The RSI is around 80 and Tesla could have a mild pullback, especially if the broader market corrects in future weeks/months.

Nonetheless, we have to put things in perspective. Elon bought more than $1 billion worth of Tesla's stock in a price range of $371.38-396.54 per share, very recently.

The Bottom Line: We Should Read Between The Lines

This dynamic illustrates that Elon has a very high conviction in Tesla's stock going much higher, in my view. Also, this dynamic also potentially limits the downside risk. If Tesla's stock temporarily dips below $400 Elon could buy more shares.

Also, the last time Elon purchased a significant number of shares on the open market, it was about 200,000 shares worth roughly $10 million at that time, in February 2020 (about $50 per share split adjusted), right before Tesla's stock exploded higher, posting about an 8X gain in the next 20 months or so.

This time, he purchased 2.57 million shares worth about $1 billion. This appears like a pretty solid signal that Tesla's stock could do extremely well as we advance.

Also, please consider this. In order for Elon to have sufficient control over Tesla, he would likely need to own 25% of the company's shares, and even with his recent purchase, his share of the company's outstanding shares is only about 13%.

While I am speculating here, the recent $1B purchase could be the beginning of a wider purchasing plan to potentially increase his stake in Tesla to that crucial 25% mark.

This dynamic may suggest that Elon could make more open market purchases, simultaneously limiting the near term downside potential, and increasing the probability for a much higher stock price as we advance.

Granted, he would have to spend a whopping $170 billion or so (with Tesla's current stock price) to reach the 25% goal. However, please keep in mind that Elon paid $44 billion, essentially out of the blue to buy Twitter.

He is the richest man in the world, and his net worth could be around $430 billion now. Therefore, I would not put it past him, to gradually accumulate Tesla's stock to increase his stake in the company, in order to increase his control.

Where TSLA Stock Could Be Headed
