I am using relatively modest estimates, in my view, as many analysts seem to have relatively low estimates based on Tesla's recent troubles, underperformance, and the lack of clear visibility into future sales and profitability increases from FSD, robotics, and other segments.

Nonetheless, I believe that Tesla could report considerably higher sales and especially much higher EPS in a more bullish case outcome if it can successfully monetize FSD, AI, robotics and other considerably higher margin businesses.

Therefore, TSLA stock price could potentially appreciate to approximately $2,500-3,500 in the next 5-10 time frame under the right, bullish circumstances.

Risks to Tesla

Tesla faces various risks despite my bullish outlook. There is the risk of increased competition in the EV space. Also, FSD could encounter issues and there is competition in the robotaxi space from Alphabet's (GOOGL) Waymo and others. There are also companies involved with robotics that could give Tesla considerable competition in that space.

Also, Elon can be unpredictable, and can be perceived as a potential risk if there are political uncertainties, or other issues going forward. Tesla also arguably has a high valuation and the stock may encounter near term turbulence. Investors should consider these and other risks before investing in Tesla.

data.title: Darden Restaurants: Margin Fears Create Opportunity (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Darden (NYSE:DRI) have been an excellent performer over the past year, rallying 30% into Thursday. However, with the stock at an elevated level, there is little room for error, and mixed quarterly earnings sent the stock down ~8% in early trading Thursday. I have been rating DRI a "Hold" for some time, including my most recent write-up in June, as I balanced strong results against a full valuation. With hindsight, I should have focused more on valuation and moved shares to a "Sell" in June as the stock is down over 12% since then. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit shares.

Q1 results were mixed, despite ongoing sales growth

In the company’s fiscal first quarter, Darden Restaurants earned $1.97 per share, which was $0.04 below expectations even as revenue grew by 10.4% to $3 billion. The company generated $439 million in adjusted EBITDA, up about 12% from last year. This remains a solidly performing business that continues to gain market share even as spending at restaurants slows. Indeed, same-restaurant sales were up 4.7%.

This strength is concentrated at its two largest brands. Olive Garden led the way, up 5.9% on a same-restaurant basis. LongHorn also continues to perform well, up 5.5%, though this represents a slowing from the past two years. Fine dining continues to be a weak spot, down 0.2%. Business and event spending remains muted. In a cost-conscious environment, travel & entertainment is a comparatively easy lever for businesses to pull back on to reduce spending, and this has been an ongoing headwind for fine dining that I do not see abating anytime soon.

Beyond same-restaurant growth, Darden continues its methodical expansion plans. Excluding its acquisition of Chuy’s, its restaurant count is up 17. I expect that to accelerate a bit, given 65 planned openings this year. Underlying growth in LongHorn and Olive Garden has been offset by declines in Bahama Breeze, which is down to 28 locations from 44. This brand will either be sold or locations converted to other Darden brands, and any proceeds from this ongoing divestiture can further support spending on new locations or share repurchases. This brand never really gained traction, and the company is increasing focus on brands with momentum, like Olive Garden and LongHorn. Together, Olive Garden and LongHorn are about two-thirds of the business.

Frankly, the level of sales growth Darden is generating continues to impress me. As you can see below, spending at restaurants has been tepid for several years now, essentially running under 2% since late 2023. There was a modest tick-up in Q2, but I view this as more likely to be quarterly volatility than a renewed upswing. With a mixed labor market and slow income growth, restaurant spending is likely to be muted going forward. I do not expect outright declines, given my view that the United States can avoid recession. Restaurants are also less exposed to tariffs than goods-producing industries, and to an extent, tariffs may shift consumption patterns away from goods and towards services.

Margins are a key focus

The concern from this quarter has less to do with sales activity and more to do with margins. Operating margins expanded by 20bps to 10%, thanks to lower corporate G&A costs. Restaurant-level margins were down 10bps due to higher labor costs. Menu pricing actions have been outpacing food inflation.

As you can see below, margins faced pressure across most business lines, with LongHorn facing the most pressure. Beef has been one of the more challenging commodities. LongHorn and Fine Dining are more exposed to this than its other businesses, which is a source of margin pressure. Its minor brands are showing some improvement as DRI integrates its Chuy’s acquisition and can remove duplicative costs and improve operating efficiencies.

Over the next six months, the company expects mid-single-digit inflation, and it has already locked in about 50% of its commodity costs. As you can see below, food input costs have been steadily rising, and they are now matching restaurant output inflation. This convergence is now pressuring margins after a multiyear period of margin expansion. With input and output costs now aligned, I do not see much more scope for margin expansion, though this should not be a cause of meaningful erosion, absent further input price increases. One potential offset is a weakening labor market may reduce wage inflation.

Guidance reflects more muted margins

Still, the impact of higher food costs is evident in how Darden management now sees the rest of the year playing out. Relative to its initial guidance, management has bumped up the sales growth range by 0.5% and the low end of its same-restaurant sales by 0.5%. However, offsetting this topline revenue benefit, it expects inflation to run 0.5% hotter than previously planned. As such, it kept its EPS guidance unchanged, as this higher revenue will be consumed by costs, rather than flow down to the bottom line. Given a fairly tepid consumer environment, I believe that further cost inflation could prove harder to mitigate with price increases without having a negative impact on traffic.

It is also important to note that Darden remains a highly cash generative business. It generated $168 million of free cash flow in the quarter despite $34 million of working capital needs. Darden also has a solid balance sheet with $211 million of cash and $2.3 billion of debt. It also has $3.9 billion of long-term operating leases. This is a modest debt load, and as a result, the company can focus on capital returns. Its share count is down about 1% from last year as it did $183 million of buybacks in the quarter. It also pays a 3% dividend yield.

Shares have pulled back enough to merit a "Buy"

Back in June, I was targeting $10.60-$10.90 in earnings this year. I would also emphasize we will see a $0.20 benefit from its fiscal year having an extra week this year; I believe it is important to adjust for this when valuing DRI. I was previously optimistic about the scope for a bit of margin expansion that I now view as unlikely to occur. That said, I expect its average share count to be lower than current guidance given ongoing repurchases. Accordingly, I am now looking for $10.60-$10.75 in EPS, or about $10.45 in a normal 52-week year.

At ~$190, shares are now about 18.2x earnings and offer a ~5% free cash flow yield. With this pullback, I now view valuation as less risky, especially as I continue to see DRI as a premier operator with two brands that are performing quite well. I also continue to favor restaurants as a relatively safe exposure to consumers amidst tariff uncertainties. With long-term growth of ~5+%, aided by its ongoing international expansion, I see shares as attractive. I would be a buyer and expect shares to rebound to the $200-$210 level, or 19x-20x. I am upgrading DRI to a "Buy."

data.title: Nvidia And Intel: Beauty And The Beast Team Up
data.description:
Well folks, it's a Thursday morning bombshell as markets open in the wake of announcements from Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) that the companies have inked a partnership to package the former's graphics chips with the latter's CPUs in processors that sport Nvidia's proprietary NVLink tech. As part of the deal, Nvidia will also take a $5 billion equity stake in Intel, which has sent INTC soaring, up 25% as I write this. This is a major upward catalyst for Intel at a time when the company was in dire need of a win, and for Nvidia this gives it easy access to x86 CPUs and provides additional rapport with the U.S. government.

I don't think this is a match many saw coming, but it certainly presents interesting synergies between the two chipmakers and has opened up new possibilities at a time when AI is pushing the processor sector to rapidly evolve. I think it'd be useful to break this partnership down by looking at how it benefits each company. Let's start with Intel.

It's no secret that Intel has been struggling to deliver on turnaround promises as its foundry continues to languish without external customers and its processors fall behind Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) in performance and power efficiency. The company has pinned a lot of its prospects on the success of its 18A manufacturing node, but has seen mixed results and is already pushing some resources towards the development of next-generation 14A instead. In the meantime, AMD has been using TSMC's (TSM) latest nodes to great effect, stealing market share, and especially revenue share, away from Intel. What better way to turn things around than to team up with the most prolific processor seller of our time.

In the enterprise space (i.e., data center), Intel has agreed to provide custom CPUs that will be packaged up with Nvidia's leading-edge AI accelerator GPUs and connected using NVLink to provide fast and efficient chips in a space that Intel has been significant losing ground. The data center market offers higher margins and higher volumes, which Intel desperately needs as its foundry business continues to bleed cash flow.

In the consumer market, Nvidia has agreed to package GPU chiplets into Intel x86 CPUs to offer an integrated SOC for PC applications, which is another area where AMD has been eating Intel's lunch as the former's Zen 5 and 3D V-Cache enabled CPUs have dominated the market in recent years.

Lastly of course, the $5 billion equity investment is no small gesture as it amounts to around 4% of the company. The purchase is subject to regulatory approval of course, but considering the U.S. government is now a significant owner of Intel stock, it seems highly unlikely that any regulatory hurdles would pose an issue.

Which leads nicely into the next topic of discussion, the pros of this partnership for Nvidia. As the most valuable company in the US, the overall dollar effect of this deal will have a substantially lower impact than it does for Intel, but it still provides some interesting benefits. The one I consider to be the most important is that it makes Nvidia and the U.S. government bedfellows at a time when the Trump administration seems poised to take a more active role in the economy and the chipmaking sector in general in the interest of national security.

While the U.S. government paid around $20 for its 10% stake in INTC, Nvidia is paying around $23 per share, which, along with 25%+ jump on Thursday, has set up an attractive cost basis and will score some political points with White House. Following the initial catastrophe of the tariff war and the Trump administration's threats to impose semiconductor tariffs on Taiwan and other countries, it's good business for the company to keep ties amicable any way it can. A joint investment in an entity that is vital to the U.S. national interest is a savvy move in that context.

The partnership also brings Nvidia closer to the U.S. following China essentially banning its domestic companies from purchasing Nvidia's H20 GPUs in order to encourage the use of homegrown AI accelerators. As the AI arms race escalates, the political aspect of operating in the market becomes more significant and with this Intel deal, Nvidia is positioning itself masterfully, in my opinion, to continue to reap the benefits.

As another benefit, packaging Intel CPUs with its AI chips, gives Nvidia a foothold in the eternal and undying x86 market, which has proven hard to break into as the Intel and AMD duopoly has posed significant barriers to entry. As Intel has been attempting to develop and refine its own GPU offerings, these barriers have remained, but it appears with this deal that Intel might be admitting defeat on that front and now sees more long-term benefit from allowing Nvidia in and collaborating than in competing.

The integration of Nvidia GPUs and Intel CPUs into a single SOC connected by NVLink has the potential to pose a threat to AMD as these chips will likely have low latency and power efficiencies that are impossible when the components are sold and packaged discretely.

Despite these synergies and benefits to both companies, there are some unanswered questions here. First, the deal makes no indication that these chips will be manufactured at Intel's foundry (though the CPU part presumably would, assuming 18A is up to snuff) and without an external customer it remains uncertain whether the foundry will survive. Will these chips be manufactured using TSMC? Even if that might be in Intel's and Nvidia's best interest, the downside of being a joint partner with the U.S. government is that the foundry will likely get priority despite its current status as a money pit.

Second, while developing chips together is all well and good and is an indicator of future collaboration, the actual volume that will result from this deal remains undetermined. Will this truly be Nvidia and Intel's flagship offerings in the x86 space? Will demand for these chips materialize? Will 18A (and eventually 14A) reach yields where mass production is even feasible? And how will Intel balance the interests of profitability alongside the U.S. government's interests that the foundry remain operational by any means necessary?

It's certainly a messy web, but overall I think this partnership results in more benefits than problems for both companies.

Investor Takeaway

Despite some uncertainties, I think this partnership has the potential to truly ignite Intel's turnaround plans as it now has major partners with deep pockets invested in its success. I have advocated for a long time that Intel should keep its foundry and rebuild through manufacturing success, and while it's not exactly the path I envisioned, the company appears to be on a favorable trajectory in this vein. I continue to see INTC as a medium-risk, high-reward play and am re-iterating a Buy even as the stock has risen 50% since my last article.

For Nvidia, I think this deal provides insulation from a lot of the political whirlwind that the company has been caught in multiple times over the past year -- becoming a joint partner with the U.S. government in a sector that has proven important to the President's agenda for domestic manufacturing for just a $5 billion investment is a stroke of genius, especially when Nvidia has so much cash to spend.

Besides being in the good graces of the U.S. government, this partnership gives Nvidia more leverage in the lucrative x86 market and provides another potential avenue for growth after China has turned off the spigot. Despite valuation concerns, NVDA has proven resilient and the AI market has continued to roll right along as GPUs remain the hottest commodity around. However, with AI reaching a fever pitch, I think investors should wait for a better entry point on the stock so, even with the pros of this Intel-Nvidia partnership, I am rating NVDA a Hold.

Thanks for reading!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Apogee Enterprises: Even In Light Of Recent Weaknesses, It Will Be Fine
data.description:
Sometimes, even when bullish calls do work out, they take a while to do so. Sometimes, you can expect to underperform the market for a time. A good example of one of my calls that has recently been underperforming is Apogee Enterprises (NASDAQ:APOG), a business that produces and sells windows, curtain walls, high-performance glass, and other similar offerings, with a focus on the non-residential space. Back in May of this year, I ended up reaffirming the company as a "Buy" candidate. Even though revenue, profits, and cash flows, were all showing signs of weakness, I believed that the long-term picture would be positive. Shares of the business were also attractively priced on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. But since then, the stock is up only 6.7%. That's barely more than half the 11.8% rise that the broader market experienced during this time.

Lately, fundamental performance has been mixed, with top-line results improving, but bottom-line results worsening. The good news is that management expects revenue to improve this year. But adjusted net profits are still forecasted to decline. To be honest with you, I believe that further weakening is possible. But in the long run, I firmly believe that the company will do just fine. And with how the stock is valued on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, I think that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate is the right move.

Still bullish

Lately, Apogee Enterprises has faced some pain. For its most recent quarter, which is its first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, the business reported sales of $346.6 million, which represents an increase of 4.6% compared to the $331.5 million reported one year earlier. According to management, this rise in revenue was driven by a $22 million benefit that the company got from its acquisition of UW Solutions, which management acquired in November of last year in exchange for $240.9 million in cash. But on a net basis, cost shareholders $232.2 million. Without this, revenue would have actually dropped year over year.

When it comes to the different operating segments of the company, the real driver was the firm’s Performance Surfaces segment, which saw revenue shoot up 99.3% from $21.2 million to $42.3 million. But again, this was because of the aforementioned acquisition. Actual organic revenue dipped by $1 million because of unfavorable product mix and broader market weaknesses. Other operating segments for the company largely weakened as well. The one exception to this was the Architectural Services segment, which reported a 7.6% increase in revenue from $99 million to $106.5 million. According to management, the segment benefited from higher volumes. But beyond that, there really wasn't much in the way of details.

The Architectural Metals segment, meanwhile, reported a 3.4% drop in revenue. When you look at the data provided, this was because of organic weakness, with revenue contracting from $133.2 million to $128.6 million. A less favorable product mix more than offset higher volumes. And finally, the Architectural Glass segment for the company plunged 15.5%, from $86.7 million to $73.3 million. This was driven entirely by weakness on the organic side, with management blaming it on lower end market demand pushing volumes down.

Profitability for the company worsened during this time. Net profits, for instance, went from $31 million to negative $2.7 million. One pain point here ended up being interest expense, which grew from $0.5 million to $3.8 million. But the biggest issue, by far, was a jump in its cost of sales from $232.7 million to $271.5 million. The company did experience during this time certain acquisition-related costs of about $7 million, as well as a small amount of restructuring expenses. But higher aluminum costs, high tariff expenses caused by the trade war situation that's going on, and an unfavorable product mix all worked against the company. It didn't help that restructuring charges of $8.4 million also negatively affected selling, general, and administrative expenses.

The good news is that adjusted net profits did come in stronger than GAAP net profits. But they still fell heartily from $31.9 million to $11.9 million. Other profitability metrics for the business also worsened. As an example, operating cash flow fell from $5.5 million to negative $19.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a worsening from $49.1 million to $27.3 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company dropped from $52.6 million to $34.4 million.

When it comes to the rest of this year, the picture does seem to be rather mixed as well. Because of the aforementioned acquisition, as well as strength regarding the Architectural Services segment, revenue is expected to come in at between $1.40 billion and $1.44 billion. That would be above, even at the low end, the $1.36 billion the company reported for last year. On the other hand, adjusted earnings per share are forecasted to come in at between $3.80 and $4.20. If the firm hits the midpoint here, that would mean adjusted net income of $85.4 million. That compares negatively to the $108.9 million the company reported the previous year. And if we continue to track declines like we have when it comes to other profitability metrics, adjusted operating cash flow would be around $129.2 million, while EBITDA would total $151.1 million.

In the event that these estimates come to fruition, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Even with the worsening expected for this year on the bottom line, shares of the company look attractively priced on an absolute basis. Furthermore, relative to other similar enterprises, shares are trading on the cheap as well. In the table below, I compared it to five such enterprises. On a price-to-earnings basis and on a price-to-operating cash-flow basis, I found that it ended up being the cheapest of the group. And when it comes down to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, only one of the companies that I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Apogee Enterprises 11.0 7.3 8.1
American Woodmark Corporation (AMWD) 12.2 10.2 7.9
Insteel Industries (IIIN) 24.7 12.8 12.0
Quanex Building Products Corporation (NX) 33.3 8.6 8.2
JELD-WEN Holding (JELD) 58.6 33.3 11.7
Janus International Group (JBI) 33.6 7.6 11.8

Near term, as the economy weakens, we could see some further pain for shareholders. Having said that, I do believe in the long-term outlook for the company. Some of this optimism centers around continued strategic actions the company is taking to boost results in the long run. In the final quarter of last year, for instance, management completed Project Fortify, which was a restructuring initiative aimed at streamlining resources in its Architectural Services and corporate/other operations. That ended up costing shareholders $16.7 million but led to annualized pre-tax cost savings of about $14 million.

And in late April of this year, management announced a second phase of this that should boost results for not only the Architectural Services segment but also the Architectural Metals segment. This plan is expected to cost shareholders somewhere between $24 million and $26 million. A lot of this will involve severance and employee-related costs as management reduces headcount. But when everything is said and done, management anticipates annualized savings of between $13 million and $15 million.

There's also the fact that the space that the company plays in is incredibly large. And, it should continue to grow over time. For instance, management estimates that the total U.S. non-residential construction space is worth around $575 billion a year. The much smaller US glazing market that includes aluminum fabrication, glass, and other materials, is a hefty $20 billion in size. This gives the farm plenty of opportunity to grow from its current size. And even though there might be continued weakness in the long run when it comes to the office space, there's plenty of opportunity when it comes to the healthcare industry, education, government agencies, lodging and recreation, airports, and even multifamily residential.

If the tariff situation persists beyond this year, we could see some continued pain. But at some point, that will come to an end. Management mentioned, for instance, that even after mitigation actions, earnings per share would be negatively affected this year by between $0.35 and $0.45. Naturally, if things continue into next year, which is very possible, we could see some additional weakness. That's especially true when you consider that the tariff situation seems to be worsening instead of getting better. So for those who can't handle a bumpy ride, looking elsewhere for potential might make sense.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I can understand why some investors might be pessimistic or cautious. I believe that Apogee Enterprises is most certainly not the best prospect out there. But it's far from a bad one. Despite the troubles the company is facing, and the risks that it faces with broader economic concerns and tariffs, shares are cheap and the long-term outlook should be positive. So if you can handle these temporary weaknesses, which admittedly could last a year or longer, I think that it will pay off. And for that reason, I've decided to keep the company rated a soft "Buy."

data.title: The Rise Of Separately Managed Accounts - Focus On Munis, September 2025
data.description:
By Patricia Healy, CFA

Cumberland has used separately managed accounts (SMAs) to execute its fixed-income strategies since the company’s inception in 1973, long before SMAs were popularized in the early 2000s.

The reasons for managing money in this fashion are the same today as they were then:

  • Transparency (you know what you own)

  • Flexibility to make strategic changes

  • Ability to manage transaction costs and best execution

  • Active management

SMAs offer individually tailored portfolio management options to address client objectives, including tax management, income production, state-specific taxation exemption, cash flow needs, and client-specific customizations. SMAs differ from pooled vehicles like mutual funds and ETFs in that each portfolio can be designed uniquely to a single investor. SMAs also differ from pooled vehicles in terms of (a) transparency, (b) containing stated maturity dates (c) liquidity management within the portfolio, and (d) flexibility for tax management, such as tax loss harvesting or customized state bias weightings.

Growth in SMAs

The advantages of SMAs have led to their increased use by investors. SMA municipal fixed-income assets, both taxable and tax-exempt, have grown from $100 billion in 2008 to an estimated $1.2 trillion of the $4 trillion market at the end of Q1 2025. SMA municipal assets have increased while direct retail and mutual fund assets have declined. Mutual funds, including money market, closed-end funds, and ETFs, totaled $1.14 trillion in muni holdings at the end of Q1 2025. SMA holdings are not separately reported in the Fed data; they are a subset of the household or retail holdings. The Q1 data that we cite is from JPMorgan and was estimated by data derived from SEC Form ADV filings by investment management firms and JPM customer surveys.

Technological improvements have also made it more efficient for managers to offer lower minimums for investing in fixed-income SMAs. At Cumberland our minimum is $250,000 and has been for some time. This size allows us to purchase enough bonds to provide diversification.

ETFs

Exchange-traded fund (ETF) volume has grown almost 73% since 2021 to over $150 billion. Although fast-growing, ETFs constitute only 3% of the $4 trillion municipal market. Conversion of mutual funds to ETFs, as well as organic growth of ETFs, has contributed to the increase. Advantages of ETFs include intraday liquidity, low cost, diversification for smaller investors, and quick exposure to the asset class for larger managers. Some investors have chosen to move into ETFs as a placeholder in anticipation of bigger supply and higher rates before reinvesting in individual bonds or SMAs.

Fund Flows

Mutual fund flows, followed regularly by the market as an indication of demand, are affected by a herd mentality and thus present opportunities for active fixed-income management. Flows into and out of mutual funds can turn very quickly. When investors are dumping assets, the mutual fund portfolio manager may not be able to fully practice active management and must liquidate funds as required by redemptions. Direct retail and SMAs tend to be stickier and, for example, may be able to take advantage of opportunities/yields when the market gets oversold.

Demand, Muni Bond Volume and Value

The muni market has experienced good demand this year even with the substantial increase in issuance. Issuance has grown from $485 billion in 2021 (having dipped to $385 billion for each of 2022 and 2023) to $508 billion in 2024. Volume for 2025 is already surpassing levels from this time last year; and by some estimates, if issuance keeps on track, volume could be $535 billion. The increase in issuance is due to numerous factors, including municipalities retaining funds to increase reserves for unseen events, funding deferred maintenance, building new infrastructure to maintain resiliency, and accommodating the changes in funding to states and projects that the federal government is considering.

Over the past 20 weeks, there have been 18 weeks of inflows into mutual funds and ETFs – an indication of demand likely reflecting higher muni yields and strong ratios. The 10y muni was at 2.93%, and the 10y Treasury was 4.06% for the week ending September 12, which results in muni/Treasury ratios of 71% for the 10y and 90% for the 30y. Depending on an investor’s tax bracket, these levels provide compelling taxable equivalent yields.

More thoughts on muni portfolio management and muni yields can be found in Ben Pease’ July 9th commentary A Forward-Looking Perspective on Municipal Bonds.

The chart below shows that cycles of inflows and outflows have occurred numerous times, and each time the recovery in rates has been quick.

Municipal Bond Credit Quality

Municipal bonds in general have higher ratings, less correlation to corporate bonds, and higher yields than other worldwide offerings do. Moody’s data show that many muni bonds are rated in the Aa category, while corporate bonds are skewed to the lower ratings, including non-investment-grade and many in the Baa category. At Cumberland we invest in taxable munis in our taxable fixed-income strategies, in addition to Treasurys, agencies, and highly rated corporate bonds.

Efficiencies

At Cumberland, we buy bonds in aggregated lots and allocate positions to individual portfolios, a strategy which may allow for better execution and pricing for our clients compared with doing individual trades for each client. When selling for individual-client money-raise requests, we use our extensive network of resources and knowledge of the markets and the particulars of a client’s portfolio to achieve best execution.

Retail accounts do not enjoy the economies of scale that are available to an SMA manager. In addition, active SMA managers that practice total-return investing may have credit-research resources and relationships with many broker dealers that allow them to achieve competitive execution and develop strategies to optimize investment holdings to meet individual clients’ needs.

While mutual fund shares can be purchased and sold any day in any amount, an SMA account has many individual holdings that may take longer to sell. However, when an investor sells shares in a mutual fund, the price received is calculated at the end of the day, based on the net asset value of the fund. If an investor is instead invested in high-quality liquid bonds like the ones Cumberland purchases in its accounts, then, barring an extraordinary event in the market, there should be ample liquidity, and the bonds could be sold at a time that achieves best execution. Additionally, knowing our clients’ needs, Cumberland is looking ahead to provide liquidity when needed. SMAs provide the advantage of customization, allowing clients the ability to determine sectors or categories to be excluded or included for a client’s desired impact, such as no tobacco-related securities or an emphasis on education or water quality. Customization is not possible with a mutual fund or an ETF.

Separately managed accounts generally have higher minimum investment requirements than mutual funds, ETFs, and robo advisors do; so they are not available to all investors. But as an investor acquires more assets and develops more highly tailored goals and objectives, an SMA may be appropriate.

Finally, the management fee charged on SMA accounts can be affected by the competitive environment. The fee is based on the type of strategy and can be scaled based on the level of assets invested. There may also be custodial fees charged to the account. Mutual funds have an expense ratio, which includes a management fee as well as miscellaneous ancillary expenses, custodial expenses, and a distribution charge. Many have various levels of sales charges. So, it is important to look at all expenses when comparing funds.

The Cumberland Approach

At Cumberland, we have a top-down approach to investment management. We look at global macroeconomic conditions and policies to assess interest rates and growth prospects to position our portfolios accordingly. Each market and/or sector is evaluated as to how it fits into the global outlook as well as how its idiosyncratic elements may affect supply and credit quality.

The majority of our fixed-income portfolios are managed on a total-return basis using a barbell strategy to take advantage of changes in interest rate and technical movements more quickly. The use of a barbell strategy allows us to invest in various short-term instruments that are liquid and to use them as ammunition to buy longer-dated bonds when interest rates rise or to take advantage of the higher coupons of longer-maturity bonds compared with shorter-dated bonds. Floating-rate notes and inflation-protected securities are investments that can help returns in the face of inflation and rising interest rates.

Returns of an account are measured against a benchmark, which is usually an index that is widely recognized. Outperformance may mean that individual portfolio returns are less negative than the benchmark’s return or that positive returns are greater than the benchmark’s.

Fixed-income total-return investing takes into consideration price appreciation or depreciation and the effects of coupon income generated and reinvested. Coupon payments over time are a large contributor to the return of an account, but the timing of buying and selling and the choice of where along the curve to buy or sell can greatly impact returns. Other buy-sell considerations include duration, or the sensitivity of a bond to changes in interest rates; embedded options such as call features; supply and demand; coupon structure; and credit-quality trends. All of these can affect the performance of a portfolio relative to an index or benchmark. Finally, to quote Cumberland’s CIO John Mousseau, “Active management means active thinking, not always active trading.”

In summary, we continue to operate as Cumberland’s founders did, investing clients’ funds in separately managed accounts. Our approach to investing is top-down and takes account of global interest rate expectations and credit quality trends as well as characteristics of each bond.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: September BoE: A Pause Amidst "Prominent" Upside Inflation Risks
data.description:
The Bank of England held rates steady in September, extending its pattern of alternating between cuts and pauses while signaling a more unified stance than in recent months. The decision reflects a balance between acknowledging the progress made in bringing inflation lower and maintaining caution as price pressures remain elevated. With growth and employment proving more resilient than expected earlier in the year, the Committee’s attention is increasingly centered on inflation risks, which continue to play a decisive role in shaping policy.

Decision

The Bank of England (BoE) decided to maintain its Bank Rate at 4.00% at the conclusion of the September meeting, continuing the pattern of alternating between cuts and pauses for the 10th straight meeting. The decision was less divided than the early August cut (which barely made it over the line), with 7 members voting for the pause and 2 members voting for a quarter-point cut. This vote was also more decisive than the June pause, which was decided with a 6-3 vote. Overall, the Bank of England’s pause today has the strongest majority since March.

In addition to the pause, the BoE’s Monetary Policy Committee [MPC] voted to reduce the stock of UK government bond purchases held for monetary policy purposes by £70 billion over the next twelve months, bringing the total to £488 billion, down from the total of £558 billion set in the September 2024 meeting. Seven members backed the £70 billion path, while Catherine Mann preferred a slightly smaller £62 billion reduction, and Huw Pill a larger £100 billion reduction, and the Committee reaffirmed that any change to the pace outside the annual review would face a high bar. This decision represents the Bank of England’s continuation of quantitative tightening, with most members aligned on the chosen pace, and only modest debate over whether the process should be somewhat slower or faster, reinforcing the Bank’s preference for a steady and predictable approach.

The focus of the statement given today is inflation, as it appears to be the main thinking keeping the MPC from cutting rates further (coincidentally, this decision was immediately preceded by a UK CPI release). The MPC starts out by pointing out the “substantial disinflation” that has occurred over the past two and a half years and explains that this “progress has allowed for reductions in [the] Bank Rate over the past year.” While inflation has generally fallen, there is still work to be done, with both CPI and core CPI annual inflation above 3.5%. The MPC continues to describe the stickiness in inflation this year as a “temporary increase,” but at the same time, it highlights that “upside risks” to inflationary pressures are a “prominent” part of the outlook.

The general stance on inflation is a bit mixed. The MPC wants to point to a trend of disinflation that “has generally continued,” but it also must voice a concern of upside risks in its economic outlook. This echoes the sentiment in the last few announcements, but interestingly, the assessment of upside inflation risks looks to be getting stronger. Specifically, we can see a difference in how it is referenced in the August statement and today’s statement:

  • August: “The MPC judges that the upside risks around medium-term inflationary pressures have moved slightly higher since May.”
  • September: “Upside risks around medium-term inflationary pressures remain prominent in the Committee’s assessment of the outlook.”

Using the word “prominent” does not feel like an accident. I believe that several members of the MPC have become very vigilant of inflationary pressures and are unlikely to vote for another cut unless inflation starts to come down again.

Why can the BoE move towards a more intense focus on inflation? A large part of it is because trends in growth and the labor market have been more resilient than they were expected to be. In the first half of the year, US tariff policy and global trade uncertainty cast a shadow over the UK economic outlook, but those factors have not been as detrimental as previously thought. Employment gains have actually been pretty solid recently, increasing 232k in the three months to July, and the unemployment rate at 4.7% is a tick below the Bank of England’s Q3 2025 forecast. GDP growth in Q2 2025 surprised to the upside, increasing 0.3% QoQ when growth was expected at 0.1% QoQ. This stronger data suggests that growth and employment are now less of a concern for the MPC than they appeared in Q1 and Q2, which is reflected in the September monetary policy statement, where the Committee devoted just three sentences to the topic.

Market Reaction

The September Bank of England decision was one of the less eventful decisions: the Bank Rate was left unchanged, and the MPC did not update the Monetary Policy Report or its economic outlook. Instead, the pause (and quantitative tightening expansion) and the statement seem to be a continuation of the gradual trend towards hawkishness that has developed as monetary policy has become less restrictive. The market’s reaction was mixed but ultimately reflected that.

  • Equities were bullish in the immediate response to the release but moved towards a more bearish trend as the trading day went on. The FTSE 100 was up around 0.2% two hours after the release, with a peak gain of 0.4% earlier. Modest gains were visible across major indexes with the exception of the riskier FTSE AIM indexes. The FTSE AIM All-Share Index fell -0.1%, and the FTSE AIM 100 index dropped -0.4%.
  • UK gilt yields started off on a dovish footing, dropping in the first 30 minutes following the decision. But the trend flipped to hawkish after that. Towards the end of the trading day, the yield curve steepened, with short-term yields dropping slightly and long-term yields seeing a more substantial rise. The largest moves were in the 10-year and 30-year gilt markets, where yields were up 4 bps in both cases.

With this decision behind us, the next question is whether the Bank of England will continue its pattern with a cut in the upcoming November meeting. Based on the trend in voting and the more intense focus on inflation, it seems like there is a good chance of the pattern breaking. The updates of inflation data over the next few months will be key for investors and policymakers in deciding whether there is any evidence of disinflation that will keep the BoE confident about continuing rate cuts.
